next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 12

Carrow was peeling an orange when they came in. He stood behind his desk with his sleeves pushed up past the forearms, digging his thumbnails into the rind and dropping strips of peel onto the wood in damp curls. The classroom smelled of citrus and, underneath it, the flat mineral odour that never left the stones anymore — scorched air, old copper, the residue of whatever had happened in here during the last lesson. The fifth-years filed in and sat along the walls.

The desks had been shoved against the stone since November, clearing the center of the room into an open floor that Gavin had stopped thinking of as a teaching space and started thinking of as a place where things happened to people. Students sat on the flagstones with their backs to the wall, schoolbags between their knees, wands out because you needed your wand out for Dark Arts or Carrow noticed. Gavin took his usual spot near the back left corner, where a torch bracket cast enough shadow that Carrow's gaze tended to skip past him. It wasn't reliable. Nothing was reliable. But the shadow felt like something, and this year Gavin took what he could get.

Duncan was already seated three places to his left. He'd been reading something when Gavin came in — not a textbook, something smaller, a folded piece of paper he'd been holding close to his face in the way of someone with a private thing in a public place. He put it away when the room started filling, tucked it into his trouser pocket with a quick, practised motion. It was a letter from home, probably. Duncan's parents wrote through the Muggle po...
open_hooks_to_carry: The quill experiment: the distinction between making something invisible (a spell applied to an object) and loosening the agreement that makes visibility apply to the object. The quill is not hidden — it is less subject to being seen. The chapter must render this precisely
The physical cost: nosebleed, visual distortion for an hour. The First Art taxes cognitive architecture, not magical energy
prev_tail_excerpt:
...o hours, and Duncan in the purple scarf because the dormitory was cold and the scarf was warm, and Malcolm Preece with his blankets pulled to his eyebrows and his one foot sticking out. The fudge tin was on Gavin's trunk with three pieces gone. The book about the demolished planet was face-down on the arm of the chair where he'd left it.

The wards were thinner than they had been.

He didn't know how he knew. The manuscript had described degradation in notation he could barely follow — terms that had no English equivalents, structural relationships he had to hold in his head like shapes in four dimensions that kept collapsing to three. But standing here with his hand still marked by the cold, he did not need the notation. He could feel it the way you feel ice thinning on a lake — not broken, not failing in any way you could point to, but less than it was. The margin between what held and what pressed had narrowed. Not by much. Not enough to see or measure or prove to anyone. But the day he had just lived — the fudge and the crackers and the sprouts and the fairy lights and the jumper that was too big and the book and the game and the scarf and the whale — all of it sat on one side of a barrier that was thinner tonight than it had been last month, and would be thinner next month than it was tonight, and nobody in the warm sleeping castle knew.

He stood at the window for a long time. The sky did not change. The pressure did not change. The stars were beautiful and precise and ancient and they did not know either.

He pulled the curtain across the window and went back to bed.
state_deltas_required: The internal pressure of weeks of failed practice reaching a tipping point, and the external impossibility of sharing the achievement with anyone who can verify it

Gavin performs his first successful First Art edit: adjusting the substrate rules governing visibility around a quill. The distinction between this and a Disillusionment Charm is the novel's central epistemological problem — the difference is invisible to anyone who cannot perceive the substrate. Physical cost: nosebleed, blurred vision
